relevant_files.role: Main SecurityScanner implementation with regex-based line-by-line scanning
PatternEngine with compiled regex patterns from embedded JSON
Core types: Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence
Smart detection logic for when to trigger security scans
SecurityConfig for ignore rules and path filtering
Integration tests including multi-line limitation test

conventions.documentation: Comprehensive rustdoc with # Arguments, # Returns, # Errors sections
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for libraries, anyhow for applications
conventions.testing: Unit tests in #[cfg(test)] modules, integration tests in tests/
conventions.code_style: must_use attributes on constructors, bon for builders
conventions.git_workflow: Conventional commits, DCO sign-off, GPG signing required
conventions.pattern_storage: JSON embedded via include_str! with compile-time loading

anti_patterns_found: Line-by-line regex scanning cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities
No AST awareness - purely textual pattern matching
No data-flow analysis between source and sink
Fixed pattern set at compile time via include_str!

related_issues.summary: Evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection - main feature request
Add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation - CLOSED
PR adding multi-line limitation test - MERGED

constraints: READ-ONLY evaluation - no code changes permitted
Must maintain backward compatibility with existing PatternEngine
Binary size increase from grammar crates is a concern
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax (learning curve)
Each language needs its own grammar crate (~5-10 languages)
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic on top of AST
test_coverage: Comprehensive test suite with unit tests in each module, integration tests in security_integration.rs, fixture-based testing with vulnerable/safe code samples, benchmark for security scanning performance. Test explicitly documents multi-line limitation via test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter
library_findings.notes: Stable, widely used, incremental parsing support

library_findings.name: regex
library_findings.notes: Fast for line-by-line, no AST awareness

approaches.title: Approach 1: Hybrid Scanner (Recommended)
approaches.summary: Keep regex PatternEngine for simple line-based patterns, add new AstScanner using tree-sitter for multi-line and language-aware detection. Both scanners implement a common SecurityScanner trait. The hybrid orchestrator runs both and deduplicates findings.
pros: Incremental adoption - no breaking changes to existing patterns
Regex remains fast for simple patterns (secrets, weak crypto)
Tree-sitter handles complex multi-line cases (SQL injection data-flow)
Can A/B test detection quality between engines
Falls back gracefully if tree-sitter fails to parse
cons: More complex architecture with two scanning engines
Need to deduplicate findings from both engines
Still need to maintain both pattern formats (regex + tree-sitter queries)
approaches.complexity: Medium - requires new trait abstraction and orchestrator, but existing code largely unchanged

approaches.title: Approach 2: Full Migration to Tree-Sitter
approaches.summary: Replace PatternEngine entirely with tree-sitter based scanning. Convert all 14 existing regex patterns to tree-sitter queries. Remove regex dependency from security scanning.
pros: Maximum detection capability for multi-line vulnerabilities
Query patterns are more readable than complex regex
Incremental parsing enables efficient re-scanning on edits
cons: Breaking change - all patterns need rewriting
Higher binary size from grammar crates
Some simple patterns may be slower than regex
Risk of parse errors on malformed code
approaches.complexity: High - requires complete pattern rewrite and extensive testing
files_to_touch: crates/aptu-core/src/security/scanner.rs - simplify to use only AST scanning
crates/aptu-core/Cargo.toml - replace regex with tree-sitter deps
All test files - update to use new pattern format

approaches.title: Approach 3: Tree-Sitter as Optional Feature
approaches.summary: Add tree-sitter as a Cargo feature flag (ast-scanning). When enabled, use AST-based detection; when disabled, use current regex engine. Allows gradual opt-in.
pros: Users can opt-in to AST scanning when ready
Smaller binary size for users who don't need AST
Can stabilize tree-sitter integration before making default
cons: Two code paths to maintain and test
files_to_touch: CI configuration - test both feature combinations

recommendation: Approach 1 (Hybrid Scanner) is recommended. It provides the best balance of capability and risk: (1) Preserves existing regex investment for simple patterns that work well, (2) Adds tree-sitter specifically for multi-line detection where regex fails, (3) Enables A/B testing and gradual migration, (4) No breaking changes to public API, (5) Can be implemented incrementally starting with just tree-sitter-rust for the spike. The key insight from #735/#736 is that the limitation is specifically multi-line detection - a hybrid approach targets this precisely without over-engineering. Start with implementing SecurityScanner as a trait, then add AstScanner for tree-sitter, finally create HybridScanner that orchestrates both with deduplication.
